summary: This lesson teaches how to analyze and make your own rules about Spanish by observing patterns. Three exercises are covered: (1) demasiado changes to match nouns but not adjectives, (2) feminine words starting and ending with 'a' in two syllables take 'el' not 'la', and (3) uno/alguno/ninguno drop the final -o before masculine nouns.

teachings.summary: Demasiado (too/too many) agrees in gender and number when modifying a noun (demasiadas casas, demasiados coches), but stays invariable as demasiado when modifying an adjective (demasiado agresivo, demasiado agresiva, demasiado agresivos).
teachings.rule: Demasiado + noun = agrees (demasiadas casas, demasiados coches). Demasiado + adjective = invariable (demasiado agresivo/a/os/as).
teachings.notes: The key is identifying word type: noun → agreement, adjective → no agreement.

teachings.conceptName: El with feminine words starting/ending in a
teachings.summary: Feminine two-syllable words that begin and end with 'a' take 'el' instead of 'la' to avoid the awkward 'la a-a' sound: el ala (the wing), el agua (the water), el aula (the classroom). They remain grammatically feminine (el agua fría, not frío).
teachings.rule: Two-syllable feminine words beginning and ending with 'a' take 'el' (not 'la'). They remain feminine for adjective agreement.

examples.english: The carpet (not affected — more than two syllables)

teachings.notes: This doesn't apply to longer words like la alfombra or la almohada — only two-syllable words beginning and ending in a.

teachings.summary: Uno (one), alguno (some), and ninguno (none) lose the final -o when placed before a masculine noun: un hombre, algún coche, ningún coche. The feminine forms (una, alguna, ninguna) do not change.
teachings.rule: Before a masculine noun: uno → un, alguno → algún, ninguno → ningún. Feminine unchanged: una, alguna, ninguna.
teachings.notes: The -o drops only before masculine nouns. When standalone (no noun following), the full form is used.

teachings.summary: The lesson emphasizes analyzing patterns in Spanish you encounter, identifying what type of word is involved (noun vs adjective), and making your own rules. Understanding why something is correct — not just memorizing — is the path to controlling the language.
teachings.notes: Identify word types (noun, adjective, verb) to figure out most grammatical behaviors. Analyze, don't memorize.

vocabulary.derivation: de (of) + más (more). Tengo cuatro de más = I have four too many.
